sourceFiles.name: editor (current)
thermal-conflict

fix.title: Introduce shared ControllableDevice supertype and distinct RecordingState
fix.description: This fix removes redundant operational-state attributes by introducing a single reusable supertype, `ControllableDevice`, that owns the canonical `deviceState : DeviceState` property. `Sensor` and `LightController` now specialize that common definition instead of each declaring the same attribute independently. `SecurityCamera` continues to inherit the shared device operational state through `Sensor`, while its camera-specific status is clarified by replacing the ambiguous `recording : DeviceState` with `recordingState : RecordingState`, making it a distinct concept rather than a duplicate of device power/operational state.

changes.description: Added a shared `ControllableDevice` supertype to own the single canonical operational-state attribute reused by multiple device definitions.
Updated `Sensor` to specialize `ControllableDevice` and removed its local duplicate `deviceState` attribute.
Updated `LightController` to specialize `ControllableDevice` and removed its duplicate local `deviceState` attribute.
Added a distinct `RecordingState` enumeration so camera recording status is modeled separately from general device operational state.
Replaced ambiguous `SecurityCamera::recording : DeviceState` with `recordingState : RecordingState` to avoid restating device operational state semantics.

results.name: 04_redundancy
results.rawText:
Model characterisation:
- System purpose: The model set describes two small systems/fragments: a SmartHome system with thermostat, lights, and security cameras for home monitoring/control, and a thermal protection fragment defining shutdown, fan, and sprinkler activation thresholds for a temperature-controlled system.
- Operational domain: Smart home / building automation for `SmartHome`; generic thermal safety/control for `thermal_system`.
- Model scope: These are partial model fragments, primarily structural definitions with a small behaviour action in `SmartHome` and requirements/attributes in `thermal_system`. They are not complete end-to-end system models.
- Known scope limitations: There are no explicit `satisfy`, `allocate`, `connect`, `bind`, package hierarchies, state machines, verification cases, or parametric models present. Checks depending on those constructs are assessed only for visible evidence and otherwise passed/skipped with rationale. Intentional architectural redundancy cannot be established unless explicitly annotated in the provided text.

CHECK PASSED: 1. Duplicate Requirements  -  The `thermal_system` package contains three distinct requirement definitions with different texts and thresholds, and no duplicated stakeholder/system requirements, derived duplicates, or repeated verification cases are present in the provided model.

CHECK PASSED: 2. Duplicate Block or Part Definitions  -  The part definitions (`Sensor`, `Thermostat`, `LightController`, `SecurityCamera`, `CoolingSystem`, `SprinklerSystem`, `System`, `SmartHomeSystem`) are distinct in name and content; no duplicated interface or near-identical block definitions were found.

CHECK PASSED: 3. Redundant Relationships  -  No explicit `satisfy`, `allocate`, `connect`, `bind`, or duplicated specialization/generalisation relationships are present in the model text, so no redundant relationship instances were found.

CHECK PASSED: 4. Redundant Behaviour Models  -  The model contains only one simple action definition (`MonitorHome`) and no duplicate state machines, repeated message patterns, or duplicated action sequences elsewhere.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-001]
Element(s):     SmartHome::Sensor::deviceState; SmartHome::LightController::deviceState; SmartHome::SecurityCamera::recording
Check:          5. Redundant Attributes & Constraints
Finding:        Device operational state is modeled repeatedly as separate attributes across multiple device definitions instead of being reused through a common general definition or clearer specialization/redefinition structure. `LightController` duplicates the same `deviceState : DeviceState` pattern already used in `Sensor`, and `SecurityCamera` adds `recording : DeviceState`, which appears to restate device operating state using the same enumeration rather than a distinct recording-specific type.
Evidence:       `part def Sensor { ... attribute deviceState : DeviceState; }`; `part def LightController { attribute brightness : Percentage; attribute deviceState : DeviceState; }`; `part def SecurityCamera :> Sensor { attribute resolution : DeviceName; attribute recording : DeviceState; }`
Score:          0.78  -  Minor-to-moderate redundancy risk because common state semantics are repeated in multiple places, increasing maintenance burden if state modelling evolves.
Confidence:     MEDIUM  -  `Sensor::deviceState` and `LightController::deviceState` are explicit duplicates, but whether `SecurityCamera::recording` is truly redundant or intentionally distinct depends on author intent not stated in the model.
Risk:           Repeated state attributes can diverge semantically over time, causing inconsistent interpretations of what "on/off/standby" means for different devices and complicating analysis or reuse.
Recommendation: Introduce a shared supertype for controllable devices or reuse a common state property consistently; keep one canonical operational-state definition and only add a separate camera recording status if it uses a distinct type or documented meaning.

CHECK PASSED: 6. Redundant Decomposition  -  No empty packages, pass-through decomposition hierarchies, or parent actions containing only a single sub-action were found in the provided fragments.

CHECK PASSED: 7. Unintentional vs. Intentional Redundancy  -  No duplicated architectural elements indicative of deliberate fault-tolerance redundancy were found; the only identified redundancy is modelling-level attribute repetition and it is not annotated as intentional design redundancy.

ISSUE [REDUNDANCY-002]
Element(s):     thermal_system::EmergencyShutdown; thermal_system::CoolingFanActivation; thermal_system::SprinklerActivation; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::emergencyShutdownThresholdC; thermal_system::CoolingSystem::coolingFanStartThresholdC; thermal_system::SprinklerSystem::activationThresholdC
Check:          8. States that are never activated
Finding:        The model defines activation logic for cooling fan and sprinkler at thresholds above the emergency shutdown threshold, making those later behaviours unreachable once shutdown occurs. These later activation conditions are therefore redundant in the behaviour implied by the requirements and attributes.
Evidence:       Requirements: `EmergencyShutdown` = "enter emergency shutdown when temperature reaches 80 degrees Celsius"; `CoolingFanActivation` = "cooling fan shall turn on when temperature reaches 85 degrees Celsius"; `SprinklerActivation` = "sprinkler system shall turn on when temperature reaches 90 degrees Celsius." Attributes: `emergencyShutdownThresholdC : Real = 80.0;`, `coolingFanStartThresholdC : Real = 85.0;`, `activationThresholdC : Real = 90.0;`
Score:          0.42  -  Significant defect because the model encodes redundant/unreachable activation conditions that are likely to mislead analysis of thermal response behaviour.
Confidence:     HIGH  -  The threshold ordering is explicit in the provided requirements and attributes; no external assumption is needed to see that shutdown at 80 precedes activations at 85 and 90.
Risk:           Analysts may assume fan and sprinkler mitigation are part of the thermal response when, under the stated logic, they can never occur, leading to incorrect safety or performance conclusions.
Recommendation: Resolve the conflict by either lowering fan/sprinkler thresholds below shutdown, redefining shutdown semantics so downstream actions remain possible, or removing the unreachable activations if shutdown truly precludes them.

Summary Table

| ID | Score | Confidence | One-line description |
|---|---:|---|---|
| REDUNDANCY-001 | 0.78 | MEDIUM | Device state is modeled repeatedly across multiple device definitions rather than through a single reusable abstraction. |
| REDUNDANCY-002 | 0.42 | HIGH | Fan and sprinkler activations are unreachable because emergency shutdown occurs at a lower temperature threshold. |

Overall Assessment Score: 0.60  
Model Status: Significant issues — not suitable for baseline or verification activities.
